Farn Polrrrcal PRACrrcEs CouurssroN
428 J Street o Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
March 12,2007
Eugene Whitlock
Deputy County Counsel
County of San Mateo
Flall of Justice and Records, 6m Floor
400 County Center
Redwood City, Califo rnia 9 4063 - | 662
RE: Your Request for fnformal Assistance
Our File No. I-07-025
Dear Mr. Whitlock:
This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of the Honorable
Julie Borden, Board Member of the Hillsborough City School District (,.District,,),
regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform eci ltfre *Act;1.1
Because the nature of your question is fact-sensitive, and thus not appropriate for formal
written advice, we provide you with informal assistance.2
QUESTIONS
1. Does Board Member Borden have a conflict of interest under the Act if she
makes, parlicipates in making, or attempts to use her official position to influence the
consideration or approval of a proposed plan before the District regarding renovation and
construction of West Hillsborough Elementary School which is 35b feetiway from her
residence?
2. If Board Member Borden has a conflict of interest, would the..public
generally" exception under the Act permit her to participate in the District,s decisions
regarding the renovation or construction of the West Hiilsborough Elementary School?
' Govern-"rt Code sections 81000 - g1}l4. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
formal written advice. (Section 83114; regulation 18329(c)(3), copy errclosed).


File No. I-07-025
Page No. 2
CONCLUSIONS
1. Because Board Mernber Borden owns real property within 500 feet of West
Hillsborough Elementary School, the financial effect of oirt i.t decisions regarding the
elementary school on the value of her residence is presumed to be material. I{owever,
this presumption may be rebutted by proof that it is not reasonably foreseeable that the
goveflrmental decision will have any financial effect on the Board Member,s residence.
Reasonable reliance on an appraisal by a disinterested and qualified real estate
professional, if based on an accurate understanding of the pirtinent facts and
circumstances, including the factors listed in regulation nlos.z6xlxA)-(c), will
generally be sufficient to rebut this presumption.
2' Assuming Board Member Borden has a conflict of interest regarding decisions
affecting West Hillsborough Elementary School, the "public generally',-ex""pfror, (Step
7) may permit Board Member Borden to participate irthe declision, dlspite h^er conflict
of interest, if either (l) l0% or more of all property owners or homeowriers in the
District, or Q) 5,000 property owners or homeowners in the Diskict would be affected,
and the governmental decisions in question would affecther economic interest in
substantially the same manner as it would affect the significant segment identified.
FACTS
Julie Borden is a member of the Governing Board of the Hillsborough City
School District ("District'). Board Member Bordin owns her family,s resijence located
at 340 Alberta Way in Hillsborough, California. It is a one-story, single family residence
in a residential neighborhood. Board Member Borden's interest in her house has a value
exceeding $2,000. As indicated in an independent appraisal report by Enright &
company, Inc. [File: 07-549] dated January 2oo7 $i;*EnrighiReport,), wirich you
enclosed with your request for advice, most residences in the goard Member,s immediate
neighborhood have lot sizes and conditions similar to her residence.
The nearest boundary of Board Member Borden's residence is 350 feet from the
nearest boundary of West Hillsborough Elementary School (the "Elern entary School,),
one of the District's four school sites. Your letter provides no information rlgardirrg it "
location of any other District school campuses relative to the Board Member,s house.
In November 2002, District voters approved a $66.8 million general obligation
bond measure for school renovation and construction, including worf at the Elernentary
School- The District's Board will also considerrevising their District-wide Master plan
and, specifically, a building strategy for the Elernentary School. you write that the
District is studying the following renovation options for the Elementary School,
scheduled to commence in the suflrmer of 20a1: (r) ADA upgrades (inierior and
exterior), and' (2) renovation of classrooms, for a total estimiied cosi of $6 million.


File No. I-07-025
Page No. 3
For 2008, the District is considering adding four additional classrooms and the cost is
estimated to be between $2 million and $4 million.
The Enright Report, on page four, states that "[b]ased on the analyses presented
within this appraisal consultation report, and subject to the assumptions and limiting
conditions, to follow, it is concluded that renovation of the Wesi ttit*Oorough
Elementary school would not have anyfinancial 
"ff"aa on the subject prop)rq.,,
(Emphasis in original.) Neither the Enright Report or your letter contains iiformation
regatding, e.g', the number of property owners or residential property owners in the
District, or the median residential lot size in the District.
ANALYSIS
The primarypurpose of the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions is to ensure that
"[p]ublic officials, whether elected or appointed, [should] perform their duties in an
impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own finantid interests or the financial
interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 81001O).) In furtherance of
this goal, section 87100 of the Act prohibits a public official from makin g, participating
in making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influenc" u gJr;rr"rtut
decision in which the official has a financial interest.
Determining whether a conflict of interest exists under section 87100 requires
analysis of the following questions as outlined below.
Steps One and Two: Is the individual a "public official, and will he be
making, participating in making, or attempting to influence a government decision?
Board Member Borden is considered a "public official" that makes, participates in
making, or atternpts to use her official position to influence governmental dicisions in her
role as a school board member.s
A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting within the authority
of his or her position, votes on a matter, obligates or commits his or her agency to any course of action, or
enters into any contactual agreement on behalf of his or her agency. lSeJtion SztOd ."& i1nzJ.) I
public official "participates in making a governmental decision" when, acting within the authority of his or
her position and without sigrificant substantive review, the official negotiatei, advises or makes
recommendations to the decision maker regarding the governmental dJcision. (Section gTlO0; reg.
l87W'2.) A public official is attempting to use fus or her official position to influence a decision before his
or her own agency if, for the purpose of influencing the decision, the offrcial contacts o, upp"*. before any
member, officer, employee, or consultant of his or her agency. (Section g7100; reg. nlo)3.y


File No. I-07-025
Page No. 4
step Three: rdentify the public officialrs economic interests.
Section 87103 provides that apublic official has a "financial interest,, in a
govemmental decision "if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generallS on the
official, a member of his or her immediate family," or on any of tfr" official,s-economic
interests, described as follows:
o A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a
direct or indirect investmenta of $2,000 or more (section 871'03(a);regulation
18703.1(a)); or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or
holds anyposition of management (section s7103(d); reg.lg703.lO)i
o A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she has a
direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more (section szro16;; reg. 1g703.2);
o A public official has an economic interest in any source of income, including
promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the
decision (section 37103(c); reg. 18703.3);
o A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the
gifts aggregate to $360 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (section
87103(e); reg. I 8703.4);
o A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal finances, including
those of his or her immediate family - this is the "personal financial effetts,, rule
(section 87103; reg. 18703.5).
Board Member Borden has an interest in her real property worth at least $2,000.
Therefore, she has an economic interest in her real property. You have not identified any
other possible economic interests associated with this orn"iut.
a An indite"t inveshnent or interest means any investnent or interest owned by the spouse of an
official or by a member of the official's immediate family, by an agent on behalf of a prUH" oin"iul, or by
a business enti[i or kust in which the offtcial, the officiai's immediate family, o. th.i, ug"nt* own directly,
indirectly, or beneficially a 10-percent interest or greater. (Section Bilo3.) i'immediate"famiiy,, is defined
at section 82029 as an official's spouse and dependent children.


File No. I-07-025
Page No. 5
Step tr'our: For each of the public official's economic interests, determine
whether that interest is directly or indirectly involved in the governmental decision
which the public official wiII be making participating in making or using or
attempting to use her official position to influence.
Once an official identifies an economic interest, he or she must determine whether
it is o'reasonably foreseeable" thatthe decision(s) in question will have a,,mate;1al
financial effect" on that interest. First, the official must decide whether the economic
interest is directly or indirectly involved in the specific decision in question. (Reg.
18700(b)(4).) Having established the degree of involvement, the official can then
identifu the materiality standard appropriate to the circumstances. (Reg. 1g700(bx5).)
The official then knows what financial effect would be considered *malerial,, under the
Act. Finally, the official must decide whether such a material financial effect is a
"reasonably foreseeable" consequence of the decision(s) at issue. (Reg. lg700OX6).)
Real Properly: Real property in which a public official has an economic interest
is directly involved in a governmental decision if for example:
"(1) The real property in which the official has an interest, or any part of
that real property, is located within 500 feet of the boundaries 1or itre
proposed boundaries) of the property which is the subject of the
governmental decision. Forpurposes of subdivision (aX5), real property
is located 'within 500 feet of the boundaries (or proposed boundaries) of
the real property which is the subject of the govemmental decision, ii arry
part of the real property is within 500 feet of the boundaries (orproposed
boundaries) of the redevelopment project area.,, (Regulation'tt;loi.z1a1.1
Board Member Borden owns a residence, the border of which is 350 feet away
from the nearest boundary of the Elementary School. Thus, pursuant to regulation
18704.2 (a)Q), her real property is directly involved in the decision(s) in qiestion.
steps Five and six: Materiarity and Reasonable Foreseeability.
Step Five asks whether a conflict of interestmay arise only when the reasonably
foreseeable financial effect of a govemmental decision on a publit official,s economic
interest is "material." (Reg. 1s700(a); see also regs. 1g705: 1g705.5.)
Step Six asks whether an effect on an economic interest is considered ..reasonably
foreseeable" if there is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Reg. 1g706(a).) A
financial effect need not be certain to be considered reasonably foresJeable, but it must
bemorethanamerepossibility. (InreThorner(1975) l FppaOps. l9g.) Butfirst,we
apply the Step Five analysis.


File No. I-07-025
Page No. 6
When real property is directly involved in a governmental decision, as here, there
is a presumption under the Commission's regulations that the financial effect of the
decision upon the real property is material. (Reg. 1g705.2(a)(t) and (a)(2).) This
presumption can only be rebutted by proof that it is not ."u.orubly for"s""a6t" that the
decisions will have any financial effect at all on the real property. (Regulation
18705-2(a)(1).) The magnitude of the financial effect is irrJevant. If fiere is any
financial effect at all (i.e., even "one penny'), the effect is presumed to be ..mat enal.,,
Because Board Member Borden's residence has been found to be directly
involved under Step Four of the analysis, the financial effect of the gor."o**tA
decision(s) being contemplated on her residen ce arepresumed to be rnaterial. However,
the determination as to whether the materiality presumption is rebutted (and whether a
material financial effect on an economic interest is reasonably foreseeable) is based upon
the presence or absence of certain facts. Therefore, these are-facfual q.r".iion, that the
public official is ultimately responsible to decide.
An appraisalby a disinterested and qualified real estate professional, based upon
an accurate understanding of all pertinent facts and circumstances, including those listed
asfactors in regulation 18705.2(bXlX4)-(C),5 will generally be consideredi good faith
effort by a public official to assess the financial effect of a decision on his or her real
property, sufficient to rebut the presumption of the regulation lg7o5.2(a)(l).
(Wainwrighr Advice Letter, No. 4-03-179; Wallac, Adri"" Letter,No. 4-03-0 69; Vadon
Advice Letter, No. 4-02-080.) The Act does not require a theoretically exhaustive or
perfect appraisal. (Jenkins Advice Letter, No. 4-99-153.) The pr"rffition can be
rebutted only when the appraisal concludes that there wili be no financial effect on the
ofEcial's real property, as the appraiser has concluded in Board Member Borden,s case.
However, a public official may not simply rely on a third-party appraisal without
further inquiry into the qualifications of the appraiser, whether t e or rrr" 
"onsidered the
factors listed in our regulations, and wheth"r th" conclusions reached by the appraiser are
objectively defensiblg i.e., based on a full and accurate assessment of att pertinent facts
and circumstances. M"-o a third-party appraisal meets these standards, a'public official
may rely on the appraisal to conclude that the presumption of regulation ttlOS.Z1a11t1
has been rebutted.6
5 The facto.s listed under regulation 1s705.2(bxl) include: "(A) The development potential or
income producing potential of the real property in which thi ofiicial has an economic interest; tTl @) The
use of the real properfy in which the official has an economic interest; tll (C)The character of the
neighborhood including, but not limited to, substantial effects on: tramc, "iew, priracy, intensity of use,
noise levels, air emissions, or similar traits of the neighborhood.,,
6 Please bear in mind the important qualification that the Commission does not act as a finder of
factjn its advice giving capacity, and that the ultimate responsibility for the reasonable assessment and
disclosure of all pertinent fa9t9 rests with the public official. (See, e.g., OdermanAdvice Letter, No. A-02-
340; O'Hara Advice Letter,No. A-00-174.)


File No. I-07-025
Page No. 7
Step Seven: WiII the Governmental Decision Ifave a Financial Effect on the
Public Official's Economic Interests that is Indistinguishable from the Effect on the
Public Generally?
An official who otherwise has a conflict of interest in a decision may still
participate in the decision under the "public generally''exception. This exception applies
if a "significant segment" of the jurisdiction or the of,ficial'ielection district is affectea
by the governmental decision in "substantially the same mannetr" as it would affect the
public official, the public official may participate in the decision. (Section g7103; Reg.
18707(a).)
Real Propefi, Significant Segmenf.' Assuming the Enright Report does not
rebut the presumption of materiality found under Step Five, abovi, she 
^has 
a conflict of
interest based on her interest in her real property. Specific subdivisions of
regulation 18707.1define a "significant segment," based on the economic interest
involved. When the economic interest which gives rise to the conflict of interest is an
interest in real property, the significant segment is defined as:
"(i) Ten percent or more of all property owners or all homeowners in the
jurisdiction of the official's agency or the dishict the official represents; or
"(ii) 5,000 property owners or homeowners in the jurisdiction of the
official's agency;' (Reg. 18707.1(bXlXBXi) - (ii).)
Because you do not present any data in your letter regarding how many property
owners or home owners are in the jurisdiction of the Board Member's District, we cannot
identifu a significant segment on which to base our analysis.
Substantially the Same Manner: If you can determine that a significant segment
will be affected by a decision, it is next necessary to determine if the pullic official,s
economic interest will be affected in substantially the same manner as the rest of the
segment. If the answer is "yes," then the effect of the decision is not distinguishable from
the effect on the public generally and the public official mayparticipate in lhe decision.
(Reg. 18707(b)(4).) To assess whether a significant segment will bi financially affected
in substantially the same manner, all measurable effects from the decision must be
identified. comparing financial effects is necessarily a factual process.
As noted above, Board Member Borden has an interest in real property located
within the area. If she determines that asignificant segment ofproperiy *r"r, o.
homeowners will be financially affected by the decisions in a manner substantially
similar to the financial effect on her real property, the "public generally', exception would
apply.


File No. I-07-025
Page No. 8
Public Generally, Small Jurisdictions.' Regulation 18707.10 (which became
operative on January 13,2007) is an additional exciption to the conflict of interest rules
under the Act which public officials in small jurisdictions may use. If a public official is
found to have a conflict of interest q{er the hrst six steps of the eigfrt-step analysis, but
meets all six requirements under 18707.10(a), that official may stilipartici'pate in the
governmental decision(s) at issue. Those six requirements are:
"(1) The jurisdiction of the public official's agency has a population of
30,000 or less and covers a geographic area oft", .q.r-" miies or less;
"(2) The public ofificial is required to live within the jurisdiction;
"(3) The public official, if elected, has been elected in an arlarge
jurisdiction;
"(4) The official's property is more than 300 feet from the boundaries of
the propert\lut is the subject of the governmental decision;
"(5) Th: official's property is Iocated on a lot not more than one-quarter
acre in size or not larger tharr, 125 percent of the median residentiai Iot
size for the jurisdiction; and
"(6) There are at least 20 other properties under separate ownership
within a 500 foot radius of the boundaries of the property that is the
subject of the govemmental decision that are similarin value.,,
Since your request does not provide us with facts allowing us to apply the
above six requirernents, we cannot evaluate whether this exception would Ziptv.
Step Eight: Is Board Member Borden Legally Required to participate in the
Decision?
Section 87101permits officials otherwise disqualified from making governmental
decisions 1o participate in decisions when their participation is legally r"q,iir!a. This is
the case if a quorum can not be achieveq lvithout the participatioiof disqualified public
officials. Since you do not present us with any facts indicating that this o"rptioo -uy
apply, we do not provide an analysis of its potential application
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
Andreas C. Rockas
Senior Counsel, Legal Division
ACRjsl

is located 'within 500 feet of the boundaries (or proposed boundaries) of
the real property which is the subject of the govemmental decision, ii arry
part of the real property is within 500 feet of the boundaries (orproposed
boundaries) of the redevelopment project area.,, (Regulation'tt;loi.z1a1.1

When real property is directly involved in a governmental decision, as here, there
is a presumption under the Commission's regulations that the financial effect of the
decision upon the real property is material. (Reg. 1g705.2(a)(t) and (a)(2).) This
presumption can only be rebutted by proof that it is not ."u.orubly for"s""a6t" that the
decisions will have any financial effect at all on the real property. (Regulation
18705-2(a)(1).) The magnitude of the financial effect is irrJevant. If fiere is any
financial effect at all (i.e., even "one penny'), the effect is presumed to be ..mat enal.,,
Because Board Member Borden's residence has been found to be directly
involved under Step Four of the analysis, the financial effect of the gor."o**tA
decision(s) being contemplated on her residen ce arepresumed to be rnaterial. However,
the determination as to whether the materiality presumption is rebutted (and whether a
material financial effect on an economic interest is reasonably foreseeable) is based upon
the presence or absence of certain facts. Therefore, these are-facfual q.r".iion, that the
public official is ultimately responsible to decide.
An appraisalby a disinterested and qualified real estate professional, based upon
an accurate understanding of all pertinent facts and circumstances, including those listed
asfactors in regulation 18705.2(bXlX4)-(C),5 will generally be consideredi good faith
effort by a public official to assess the financial effect of a decision on his or her real
property, sufficient to rebut the presumption of the regulation lg7o5.2(a)(l).
(Wainwrighr Advice Letter, No. 4-03-179; Wallac, Adri"" Letter,No. 4-03-0 69; Vadon
Advice Letter, No. 4-02-080.) The Act does not require a theoretically exhaustive or
perfect appraisal. (Jenkins Advice Letter, No. 4-99-153.) The pr"rffition can be
rebutted only when the appraisal concludes that there wili be no financial effect on the
ofEcial's real property, as the appraiser has concluded in Board Member Borden,s case.
However, a public official may not simply rely on a third-party appraisal without
further inquiry into the qualifications of the appraiser, whether t e or rrr" 
"onsidered the
factors listed in our regulations, and wheth"r th" conclusions reached by the appraiser are
objectively defensiblg i.e., based on a full and accurate assessment of att pertinent facts
and circumstances. M"-o a third-party appraisal meets these standards, a'public official
may rely on the appraisal to conclude that the presumption of regulation ttlOS.Z1a11t1
has been rebutted.6
5 The facto.s listed under regulation 1s705.2(bxl) include: "(A) The development potential or
income producing potential of the real property in which thi ofiicial has an economic interest; tTl @) The
use of the real properfy in which the official has an economic interest; tll (C)The character of the
neighborhood including, but not limited to, substantial effects on: tramc, "iew, priracy, intensity of use,
noise levels, air emissions, or similar traits of the neighborhood.,,
6 Please bear in mind the important qualification that the Commission does not act as a finder of
factjn its advice giving capacity, and that the ultimate responsibility for the reasonable assessment and
disclosure of all pertinent fa9t9 rests with the public official. (See, e.g., OdermanAdvice Letter, No. A-02-
340; O'Hara Advice Letter,No. A-00-174.)
Step Seven: WiII the Governmental Decision Ifave a Financial Effect on the
Public Official's Economic Interests that is Indistinguishable from the Effect on the
Public Generally?
An official who otherwise has a conflict of interest in a decision may still
participate in the decision under the "public generally''exception. This exception applies
if a "significant segment" of the jurisdiction or the of,ficial'ielection district is affectea
by the governmental decision in "substantially the same mannetr" as it would affect the
public official, the public official may participate in the decision. (Section g7103; Reg.
18707(a).)
Real Propefi, Significant Segmenf.' Assuming the Enright Report does not
rebut the presumption of materiality found under Step Five, abovi, she 
^has 
a conflict of
interest based on her interest in her real property. Specific subdivisions of
regulation 18707.1define a "significant segment," based on the economic interest
involved. When the economic interest which gives rise to the conflict of interest is an
interest in real property, the significant segment is defined as:
"(i) Ten percent or more of all property owners or all homeowners in the
jurisdiction of the official's agency or the dishict the official represents; or
"(ii) 5,000 property owners or homeowners in the jurisdiction of the
official's agency;' (Reg. 18707.1(bXlXBXi) - (ii).)
Because you do not present any data in your letter regarding how many property
owners or home owners are in the jurisdiction of the Board Member's District, we cannot
identifu a significant segment on which to base our analysis.
Substantially the Same Manner: If you can determine that a significant segment
will be affected by a decision, it is next necessary to determine if the pullic official,s
economic interest will be affected in substantially the same manner as the rest of the
segment. If the answer is "yes," then the effect of the decision is not distinguishable from
the effect on the public generally and the public official mayparticipate in lhe decision.
(Reg. 18707(b)(4).) To assess whether a significant segment will bi financially affected
in substantially the same manner, all measurable effects from the decision must be
identified. comparing financial effects is necessarily a factual process.
As noted above, Board Member Borden has an interest in real property located
within the area. If she determines that asignificant segment ofproperiy *r"r, o.
homeowners will be financially affected by the decisions in a manner substantially
similar to the financial effect on her real property, the "public generally', exception would
apply.
Public Generally, Small Jurisdictions.' Regulation 18707.10 (which became
operative on January 13,2007) is an additional exciption to the conflict of interest rules
under the Act which public officials in small jurisdictions may use. If a public official is
found to have a conflict of interest q{er the hrst six steps of the eigfrt-step analysis, but
meets all six requirements under 18707.10(a), that official may stilipartici'pate in the
governmental decision(s) at issue. Those six requirements are:
"(1) The jurisdiction of the public official's agency has a population of
30,000 or less and covers a geographic area oft", .q.r-" miies or less;
"(2) The public ofificial is required to live within the jurisdiction;
"(3) The public official, if elected, has been elected in an arlarge
jurisdiction;
"(4) The official's property is more than 300 feet from the boundaries of
the propert\lut is the subject of the governmental decision;
"(5) Th: official's property is Iocated on a lot not more than one-quarter
acre in size or not larger tharr, 125 percent of the median residentiai Iot
size for the jurisdiction; and
"(6) There are at least 20 other properties under separate ownership
within a 500 foot radius of the boundaries of the property that is the
subject of the govemmental decision that are similarin value.,,
Since your request does not provide us with facts allowing us to apply the
above six requirernents, we cannot evaluate whether this exception would Ziptv.
Step Eight: Is Board Member Borden Legally Required to participate in the
Decision?
Section 87101permits officials otherwise disqualified from making governmental
decisions 1o participate in decisions when their participation is legally r"q,iir!a. This is
the case if a quorum can not be achieveq lvithout the participatioiof disqualified public
officials. Since you do not present us with any facts indicating that this o"rptioo -uy
apply, we do not provide an analysis of its potential application

QUESTION: 1. Does Board Member Borden have a conflict of interest under the Act if she
makes, parlicipates in making, or attempts to use her official position to influence the
consideration or approval of a proposed plan before the District regarding renovation and
construction of West Hillsborough Elementary School which is 35b feetiway from her
residence?
2. If Board Member Borden has a conflict of interest, would the..public
generally" exception under the Act permit her to participate in the District,s decisions
regarding the renovation or construction of the West Hiilsborough Elementary School?
' Govern-"rt Code sections 81000 - g1}l4. ; regulation 18329(c)(3), copy errclosed).

CONCLUSION: 1. Because Board Mernber Borden owns real property within 500 feet of West
Hillsborough Elementary School, the financial effect of oirt i.t decisions regarding the
elementary school on the value of her residence is presumed to be material. I{owever,
this presumption may be rebutted by proof that it is not reasonably foreseeable that the
goveflrmental decision will have any financial effect on the Board Member,s residence.
Reasonable reliance on an appraisal by a disinterested and qualified real estate
professional, if based on an accurate understanding of the pirtinent facts and
circumstances, including the factors listed in regulation nlos.z6xlxA)-(c), will
generally be sufficient to rebut this presumption.
2' Assuming Board Member Borden has a conflict of interest regarding decisions
affecting West Hillsborough Elementary School, the "public generally',-ex""pfror, (Step
7) may permit Board Member Borden to participate irthe declision, dlspite h^er conflict
of interest, if either (l) l0% or more of all property owners or homeowriers in the
District, or Q) 5,000 property owners or homeowners in the Diskict would be affected,
and the governmental decisions in question would affecther economic interest in
substantially the same manner as it would affect the significant segment identified.
